definition: Taking experience personally or ignorantly holding onto experience, views, habits, or identity.
notes: This entry uses taking personally as the house default. The literal force is taking near, and the project default makes the appropriating movement explicit in English. Ignorantly holding remains a close alternate when the emphasis is on sustained holding, while clinging and grasping remain available in more familiar doctrinal prose. The term should be heard as appropriation into I and mine rather than as generic emotional attachment. Translators should not substitute attachment as a default and should keep the personalizing force explicit in dependent-arising and aggregate formulae unless a compound entry records a narrower rule.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and dependent-arising contexts
In prose emphasizing sustained appropriation or holding
context_rules.notes: Use when the ongoing holding side of the term is more important than the personalizing side.

context_rules.context: In comparative doctrinal prose using more familiar Buddhist-English terminology
context_rules.notes: Acceptable alternate when continuity with established usage helps.
Acceptable when the motion of taking hold is central.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose tied to the fire metaphor
context_rules.notes: Use selectively because it is not the default doctrinal gloss.

context_rules.context: In compounds such as kāmupādāna, diṭṭhupādāna, sīlabbatupādāna, and attavādupādāna
context_rules.notes: The family should inherit the appropriative force of the headword unless a compound explicitly records a different emphasis.

example_phrases.translation: with ignorant wanting as condition, taking personally

version_notes: Strengthened to extend the appropriative rule into the upādāna compound family and to block drift toward generic attachment language.
